subject: Add model changes to support api and swagger
commit_message:
Add model changes to support api and swagger

Includes:
a) Changed properties type in ToscaPolicy.java
b) Fixed a typo in persistence.xml
c) Added swagger annotation to hide fields in swagger

Issue-ID: POLICY-1515
Change-Id: Id019886b46c20bf4cf562a1d2011bc8370b0272a
Signed-off-by: Chenfei Gao <cgao@research.att.com>

entries.message:
Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-models-master-verify-java/73/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/policy-models-master-verify-java/73

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

Using @ApiModelProperty(hidden=true) can work for hiding field in swagger UI. But this review is not finished yet. Some fields (e.g. ToscaPolicies and ToscaPolicyTypes) are very tricky as they are sub-classes of PfConceptContainer and we use class template <C extends PfConcept> in PfConceptContainer. The populated class in class template cannot be introspected by swagger-core. So the MAP structure cannot still be rendered correctly in swagger ui. 

And more tosca constraints need to be added in, e.g. valid_values, equal,... which have been used in tca policy type

Patch Set 1:

I am stuck in this now. I don't know how to filter out rest of unnecessary fields. Perhaps Liam or Ram would have better solution. I switch to work on API endpoints for legacy policies now.

Patch Set 1:

I think I want to wait for Liam to review this.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(1 comment)

We should follow TOSCA in the model even if what's coming in is not valid TOSCA. The model is designed to be as close to TOSCA as possible.

Also, I'm not sure what value hiding the fields from Swagger will have because the fields represent incoming fields (concept key -> name/version pair, and type-> policy type reference). Hiding the fields will not produce correct output on the swagger.

Patch Set 1:

Chenfei, I see your point now,. we're talking about the value of the policy property and not its type definition. I think the correct way to model this is to have it as Map<PfCOnceptKey, Object>, where the PfConceptKey parent is the policy and the local name is the name of the property. This allows the property type to be checked. The type of the Object value can then be checked in validation

Patch Set 1:

Thinking about it again, the correct mapping is Map<String, Object>, where the string is the policy name and the object is an object of the type in the policy definition. We can verify that.

Patch Set 1:

> Thinking about it again, the correct mapping is Map<String,
 > Object>, where the string is the policy name and the object is an
 > object of the type in the policy definition. We can verify that.

Liam, do you mean "private Map<String, Object> properties"? If so, I agree that is a better representation than map<String, String>. And, actually the Object here is an object of the type defined in corresponding property in "policy type" definition. Validating all of these is very tricky. Would you do that or want me to do that? I am afraid I would screw up models...

Patch Set 1:

Maybe I should abandon this review. Not sure adding @ApiModelProperty(hidden=true) can beatify all swagger payloads since some fields in subclass of PfConceptContainer cannot be rendered correctly in this way and I don't know what is the best way to do that. How do you think?
